openingMessage: Accountability doesn't just mean answering for what went wrong. It means owning your position, your zone, and your crew — before anything goes wrong.
leadershipThought: The person in the line of fire rarely put themselves there on purpose. They drifted. Stay deliberate.
opsContent:
Carry-Forward from Monday June 29:
• Gage Gonzales — Ovintiv HSC 320: resuming ops 7:00 AM this morning
• Jordan Lemmons — MT and vac truck job start not confirmed from yesterday; status needed
• Neck brace photo — unidentified crew member photographed June 29 at 1:34 PM wearing neck brace; identity and work-related context still unknown — follow up today
• Fire off HWY 176 — Ray Solis reported smoke/fire visible from the road at 4:12 PM (June 29). No crew impact confirmed, but area crews should be aware if working near that corridor

Open jobs resuming today:
• Michael Hutt / Marcos Morales — SM Energy Coonskin Flowback: trailer staged at location from Monday; confirm return-to-ops plan
• Caleb Smith — completed Shaw 183 Monday; confirm today's assignment
lessonsLearned:
• Rattlesnake on location — Ray Solis posted photos June 29 of a rattlesnake under/near equipment at 10:07 AM. Caption: "Be careful where you step." This is the season. Watch low-clearance equipment, shadow areas, and behind equipment skids before you step in. 🔴
• Something on fire off HWY 176 — Ray Solis reported and posted photos of smoke/fire visible from the road at 4:12 PM June 29. Nature of fire not confirmed (grass fire likely). If you're working near the HWY 176 corridor, note it. No crew involvement reported.
• Neck brace photo — unresolved — Crew member photographed June 29 at 1:34 PM wearing a neck brace with no context in Breakroom. If work-related, it needs documentation. Identify the individual and confirm.
exxonMoments:
Crane Work — SWA Exercised During Horse Head Install (Line of Fire)
A Canon technician observed a horse head installation using a crane at a Midkiff-area location. During the lift, unsafe conditions were identified and Stop Work Authority was exercised. Atmospheric monitoring, respiratory protection, exclusion zones, and hold points were all active when the stop was called and throughout resolution. Crane lifts place every person in the swing radius in the direct line of fire from a suspended load — SWA during the lift is the one control that covers everyone in that zone, not just the operator.

Pre-Lift Plan Before the Hook Was On (Line of Fire)
Before a crane was used at a Stanton-area well, the crew paused to discuss the lift plan, hazards, and ground crew positions before any rigging was attached. Communication was strong throughout and the location was left prepared for upcoming operations. A lift plan conversation before the hook is attached is what establishes who stands where and what happens if the load shifts — that conversation is the line-of-fire control, and it cannot happen after rigging is set.

Hydrovac Hose Burst — SWA, Repair, Full Re-Brief Before Resuming (Line of Fire)
During a job at a Midkiff-area location, a hydrovac hose burst during operations. Stop Work Authority was exercised immediately, the hose was repaired, and the crew conducted a full job scope re-review with all personnel before resuming. Full controls — LOTO, atmospheric monitoring, respiratory protection, exclusion zones, and hold points — were confirmed in place throughout. When equipment fails under pressure, the area around it becomes a line-of-fire zone instantly. Fix the equipment and re-brief the crew before anyone steps back in — the re-brief realigns situational awareness that the incident shifted.

Back-to-Back Jobs Don't Get Automatic Repeats (Line of Fire)
A Canon technician completed two consecutive horse head and flow line removals at adjacent locations on the same day. All controls were confirmed for both jobs. The post-job note: back-to-back jobs of the same type can create complacency — the second job should receive the same hold-point rigor as the first, not an assumption that it will go the same way. Horse head removal with mechanical equipment puts the crew in the swing and drop radius of heavy components. Treating the second job as a repeat of the first is how exclusion zones drift and personnel end up where they should not be.

The Safety Observer Who Rushes Isn't Observing (Line of Fire)
A new Site Safety Escort was coached on-location about LSRA requirements and hazard controls. The key coaching point: the SSE was at risk of matching crew pace rather than maintaining an independent oversight position. A safety role is not a crew role — the person moving at crew pace is reacting, not watching. You cannot be inside the work zone and observe the line of fire at the same time. Step out. Slow down. Lead from the front.

fieldNews.headline: Caleb Smith — Confined Space Certification
fieldNews.detail: Bobby Galindo shared Caleb's Republic Safety LLC Confined Space Entry/Rescue Training certificate (completed June 28, instructor Eddy Hada). Caleb came in off a Sunday training day — that is the standard.

fieldNews.headline: Dawson Drake
fieldNews.detail: SM HSE was continuously going around the Schlitz A site Monday and was impressed with everything. Two separators cleaned, crew presence and professionalism noted.

fieldNews.headline: Jacob Hernandez
fieldNews.detail: Bizzel Irvin below-grade conversion completed safely and efficiently. Atmospheric testing confirmed safe, Davit arm utilized, Co Rep released crew.

fieldNews.headline: Rattlesnake on location — Ray Solis
fieldNews.detail: Photos posted June 29 at 10:07 AM. Rattlesnake under/near equipment. Watch low-clearance and shadow areas before stepping in.

fieldNews.headline: Neck brace photo — identity unconfirmed
fieldNews.detail: Crew member photographed June 29 at 1:34 PM wearing a neck brace. No context given. Identity and work-related status still unknown — needs follow-up today.

todaysChallenge: Before your next job today, point out one specific line-of-fire risk at that location.
todaysChallengeSubtext: Not a general hazard — a specific one. Where could a load swing? Where could a valve release? Where could a vehicle turn? Name it before it names you.

compliance.footerNote: Update numbers in daily.json each Monday morning.
